1: Ps 84: Sons of Korah Pilgrim Psalm — One of the Psalter's Most Beloved Poems. Ps 84 is a pilgrimage psalm expressing longing for the Jerusalem temple and the joy of presence in YHWH's courts. The Sons of Korah guild (associated with the temple gatekeeping and music ministry: 1 Chronicles 9:19; Psalms 26:1-19) composed an ardent love-poem for the sanctuary they served. 'How lovely is your dwelling place, O LORD of hosts!' — ma yedidot mishkenotechah YHWH Tzvaot — the exclamatory ma (how!/how much!) expresses overwhelming aesthetic and spiritual delight. Mishkenotechah (your dwelling places, plural) — possibly the tabernacle precincts in their multiple areas, or the emphatic plural of the sanctuary's rich dimensions.
2: 'My soul longs, yes, faints for the courts of the LORD; my heart and flesh sing for joy to the living God' — nichsephah vegam kalethah naphshi (my soul longs, even faints with longing) — the vocabulary of intense desire: nichsephah (Niphal of kasaph, to long/yearn with physical ache) + kalethah (kalah, to be consumed, exhausted by longing, cf. Psalms 119:81). Both soul (naphesh) and flesh (basar) sing — the whole person, interior and exterior, is engaged. 'The living God' (El chai) — not a static deity of idol-cult but the dynamically active God.
3: 'Even the sparrow finds a home, and the swallow a nest for herself, where she may lay her young, at your altars, O LORD of hosts, my King and my God' — one of the most charming images in the Psalter: birds nesting at the temple altars have what the pilgrim desperately longs for — permanent, unimpeded access to YHWH's presence. The sparrow (tzippor) and swallow (deror) build nests near the bronze altars of the temple courts. The birds are not conscious worshipers, but their habitual presence near the divine dwelling is the pilgrim's envy. 'My King and my God' (malki v-Elohai) — the personal covenant appropriation at the psalm's center.
4: 'Blessed are those who dwell in your house, ever singing your praise!' — ashre yoshve veitechah — the first of three beatitudes in the psalm (vv4, 5, 12). 'Those who dwell in your house' — the Levites and priests who had permanent access to the temple precincts. The pilgrim who visits occasionally envies those who can 'ever' (od, continually) be in the presence of YHWH. The temple servants have what the pilgrim travels far to experience briefly.
5: 'Blessed are those whose strength is in you, in whose heart are the highways to Zion' — the second beatitude: the blessed person has both their strength (oz) in YHWH and the pilgrim roads (mesillot) in their heart. 'Highways to Zion' (mesillot bilvavam) — the actual pilgrimage roads to Jerusalem are internalized in the pilgrim's heart: the longing to make the journey, the mental rehearsal of the route. The roads that lead to Zion run through the heart before they run through the landscape.
6: 'As they go through the Valley of Baca, they make it a spring; the early rain also covers it with pools' — the 'Valley of Baca' (emek habaka) — the word baka means 'weeping' or may be the name of a specific dry valley on the pilgrimage route to Jerusalem. In either reading, the transformation is miraculous: a valley of weeping or dryness becomes a spring (maayan) through the pilgrims' passage. Their tears become water; their longing transforms the landscape. 'The early rain covers it with pools' — the autumn rains that begin the new year make even the dry valley overflow. The pilgrimage journey transforms both the traveler and the terrain.
7: 'They go from strength to strength; each one appears before God in Zion' — yelkhu mechayil el-chayil (they go from strength to strength) — a unique phrase: the pilgrimage journey does not exhaust but strengthens. Each stage of the journey produces more capacity for the next. 'Each one appears before God in Zion' (yera'e el-Elohim betziyon) — the covenant obligation of the three pilgrimage festivals was 'to appear before the LORD' (Deuteronomy 16:16; Exodus 23:17). The appearing (yera'eh) is the formal covenant audience with the divine king at his palace.
8: 'O LORD God of hosts, hear my prayer; give ear, O God of Jacob!' — the interlude petition: the pilgrim at the temple prays for YHWH's attention. The two divine titles: YHWH Elohim Tzvaot (LORD God of hosts — the most complete title) and Elohei Yaakov (God of Jacob — the covenant-patriarch title). The combination: cosmic power (Tzvaot) and covenant faithfulness (Yaakov) are both invoked.
9: 'Behold our shield, O God; look on the face of your anointed!' — maginenu (our shield) — YHWH as the covenant shield (Genesis 15:1: 'I am your shield'). 'Your anointed' (meshichechah) — the Davidic king. The pilgrim's prayer for the king: YHWH's attention to the king's face (panim) is a request for divine favor. The pilgrimage psalm includes prayer for the royal patron through whom YHWH's blessings flow to the people.
10: 'For a day in your courts is better than a thousand elsewhere. I would rather be a doorkeeper in the house of my God than dwell in the tents of wickedness' — the famous comparison: one day (yom echad) in YHWH's courts > 1000 days (alaphim) anywhere else. 'Doorkeeper in the house of my God' (histopheph beveyt Elohai) — histopheph (to stand at the threshold/doorway, to hover at the entrance) — the most peripheral possible position inside the temple boundary is preferable to the most comfortable position in 'the tents of wickedness' (ahale resha). The contrast: covenant poverty at the temple edge vs. covenant-free prosperity.
11: 'For the LORD God is a sun and shield; the LORD bestows favor and honor. No good thing does he withhold from those who walk uprightly' — 'the LORD is a sun' (ki shemesh umagen YHWH Elohim) — uniquely in the OT, YHWH is called a shemesh (sun). Solar imagery was intensely politically charged in the ANE: sun-gods (Ra, Shamash, the Aten) were the supreme deities of Egypt and Mesopotamia. Israel's anti-solar-cult polemic (Deuteronomy 17:3; Ezekiel 8:16) made comparing YHWH to the sun rare. Here the comparison works: as the sun gives light and warmth to all creation, YHWH gives favor (chen) and honor (kavod) to those who walk uprightly.
12: 'O LORD of hosts, blessed is the one who trusts in you!' — the third beatitude closes the psalm: ashre adam boteach bach (blessed is the person who trusts in you). The pilgrimage psalm that opened with 'how lovely' and moved through longing, transformation, prayer, and comparison closes with the simplest possible covenant affirmation: the person who trusts YHWH is blessed. Batach (trust) is the covenant posture that organizes all the other elements of the psalm.
